does: This paper introduces Implicit Preference Alignment (IPA), a post-training framework that improves hand motion generation in human image animation without requiring paired preference data. The method is grounded in implicit reward maximization, working by maximizing likelihood of self-generated high-quality samples while maintaining proximity to the pretrained model. The authors integrate a Hand-Aware Local Optimization mechanism that directs the alignment process specifically toward hand regions, addressing their high degrees of freedom and complexity. The approach eliminates the practical bottleneck of curating frame-wise consistent preference pairs for dynamic hand regions, making the method data-efficient and scalable for improving hand motion fidelity in animation generation.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and direct preference optimization (DPO), which have shown promise for aligning generative models with human preferences. The paper responds to a key limitation in applying these techniques to human image animation: the prohibitive cost of constructing paired preference annotations, especially for frame-level consistency in complex motion domains. It extends prior research on preference-based model alignment by developing an implicit variant that sidesteps explicit pair construction, drawing on implicit reward learning frameworks. The research also connects to broader efforts in video generation and motion synthesis that seek to handle high-dimensional motion spaces while maintaining temporal coherence, particularly for articulated structures like hands.
enables: This work opens pathways for more practical deployment of preference-based alignment in video synthesis and human animation tasks where annotation budgets are constrained. The implicit preference framework could extend beyond hand animation to other high-complexity motion regions or full-body animation, reducing data collection overhead. The Hand-Aware Local Optimization mechanism establishes a template for designing region-specific or part-aware alignment strategies applicable to other articulated animation challenges. The method potentially enables interactive animation systems where models improve through self-supervision rather than expensive human annotation loops. Future work could explore combining IPA with other video generation architectures, extending it to multi-person scenarios, or adapting implicit preference learning to other vision tasks requiring fine-grained temporal or spatial alignment.
